Re
Farn Polrrrcel PRACTTcEs CouurssroN
-128 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento. CA 95gt.l-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322_08{i6
June l, 2010
Michaelyn Jones
General Counsel
Santa Monica Rent Control Board
1685 Main Street
P.O. Box 2200
Santa Monica, CA 90407 -ZZOO
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-051a
Dear Ms. Jones:
This letter respo-nds to your request for reconsideration of advice regarding the conflict-
of'-interest provisions of the political Reform Act (the "Act")l provicled in o1,. previous advice
letter to yotl, on behalf of Santa Monica Rent Control Board Commissioner Robert Kronovet,
dated April29,2Ol0. (Jones Aclvice Letter, No. A-10_051.)
Please note that nothing in this letter should be constmed to evaluate any conduct that
may have already taken place, and conclusions in this letter apply only to prospective actions. [1
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Poliiical practices Commission (,,the
Commission") does not act as a fincler of fact when it renders assistance. (In re ogte.tby ( 1975) I
FPPC Ops. 71.)
For the purposes of this letter, the facts presented in your previons request fbr advice
dated April29,2009' have been incorporatecl hirein by referenc e. (Jones Advice Letter, .supra.)
You have added the following relevant facts to yoLlr request: There are 22,061property
owners in Santa Monica and20,734 residential properti o*ner.s. There are 3,036 owners of rent
controlled properties.
Yott also state that, "lt is highly unlikely that a 4.2vo increase will be grantecl this year or
at any time in the near futllre." You also statecl that since the issuance of ot,r"prior advice to yoll
that "staff has recommended a two percent increase for consideration by the Board.,, you also
' The Political Retirrm Act is contained in G()vernment Code sections tl l (xx) through g l o l 4. All statukrry
ret'erencesaretotheGovernmentcode.unlessotherwiseindicated.'IheregulationsoftheFairpolitical practiees
Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 thrrru-uh 18997 of 'litle ] of the Califbrnia Code of Rcgulations. All
Iegulakrry ref'erences are to'ritle 2, Division 6 of the Calitornia Cotle of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated_


File No. A-10-05la
Page No. 2
said it was "unlikely, if not impossible, that the Board's decision would exceed two percent.,, [f
the board approves a one percent increase, the amount of income to Commissioner Kronovet
from his six residential rental units would be $8g3.6g.2 If a two percent incre-ase is approved,
the increase to the property's income producing potential would be $1,767.363 annualiy.
In addition, you state that on May 19, 20L0, Commissioner Kronovet signed a waiver
agreeing not to take the 20LOl20l1 annual general adjustment, and that "by doiirg so, he believes
he has removed any possible conflict of interest having to do with his ownership of the six-unit
residential property. . .."
Based on the new facts provided, you ask reconsideration with regard to whether:
(l) The presumption of non-materiality for commissioner Kronovet,s real
property interest is rebutted pursuant to Regulation lg7o5.2(b) due to the
possible effects on the income-producing potential of the real property;
(2) Whether the public generally exception applies to Commissioner Kronovet,s
circumstances;
(3) whether commissioner Kronovet's waiver of the zololz}Ll General
Adjustment has any effect on his legal obligation to disqualify himself from the
annual rent increase decision.
Income Producing Potential of Real property and the waiver:
You state that Regulation 18705.2(b) "gives no indication of a threshold amount that
would rise to the standard of rebutting the presumption" of materiality with regard to an
indirectly involved property's "income producing potential." Therefore, you u-rk that the
application of the income producing criteria in this regulation "should ,oi b" applied to
Commissioner Kronovet's economic interest . . .." You also state that you do not think that the
presumption is rebutted and that "the decision regarding the annual reni increase will not have a
reasonably foreseeable material effect on Commissioner Kronovet's residential rental property.,,
Commissioner Kronovet's waiver, if valid and legally enforceable,a would have a bearing
on the income producing potential of his six residential rental property units as otherwise
permitted if the annual general adjustment is passed by the boird.- ThLrefore, based on the
waiver, the impact on Commissioner Kronovet's property may not be sufficient to rebut the
' The current combined monthly maximum allowable rents equal $7,364p1us I percent equals $73.64.
The amount $73.64 x l2 months equals .9883.68.
3 The current combined monthly maximlm allowable rents equal $7,364p1us 2 percent equals $ 147.2g.
The amount $1.17.28 x l2 months equals $1,767.36.
a Because of our ultimate conclusion that the Annual General Adjustment will still have an impact on the
l'alue of the real property, we do not otfer an opinion as to the validity or enfbrceability of the waiver as it relates to
the adjustment and the rental property.


File No. A-10-051a
Page No. 3
presumption of nonmateriality with regard to indirectly involved property. You also contend,
however, that because it is unlikely the board will approve a rental odi6i11.nt of more than two
percent, the decision on the adjustment will not reach the required maieriality threshold for
income even if his waiver is not valid or enforceable. However, without moie facts concerning
exactly how the adjustment will be presented to the board for decision, we cannot accept yollr
contention. For example, if the motion is presented to the board as a two percent adjusimlnt but
Commissioner Kronovet and other board members think the percentage should be higher, a ,.no,,
vote by Mr. Kronovet could result in a rejection of the propoied t*o ["r""rt adjustm-ent and the
imposition of a higher percentage even though he does noiparticipate in the voie on the higher
rate. Therefore, although a valid and enforceable waiver by Commissioner Kronovet may
diminish income as a factor requiring his disqualification, based on yollr letter, we do ,oi og.."
that a decision solely on a two percent adjustment removes income as a factor.
Nevertheless, as we stated in our response to Commissioner Kronovet's separate request
for reconsideration (Kronovet Advice Letter, A-10-093), it is but one factor considired in
determining whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have a material financial
effect on an official's real property interest.
In our original letter, we said the waiver, on its face, does not completely and
unequivocally neutralize the material financial benefits of the annual rent increase decision and
its effects generally on property values of rent-controlled real estate in the city. In other words,
regardless of whether Commissioner Kronovet receives additional income from the Annual
General Adjustment for 2010-}OLI, we believe the decision would still have an impact on rhe
value of his real property. Moreover, because Commissioner Kronovet owns six reiidential rental
property units, this financial effect would be substantially magnified with regard to his property
interests as compared to the financial effect on property o*r"ir with only orie property. (Enright
Advice Letter, No. A-10-006.)
Therefore, reviewing the additional facts you present and the applicable law, we reaffirm
the conclusion reached in the Jones Advice Letter, trrpro that that Commissioner Kronovet has a
disqualifying conflict of interest and may not make, participate in making, or influence any
government decisions allowing residential rental property owners in the -ity o, annual rent
increase because the decision would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on his
economic interest in six residential rental units.
P ub lic Ge ne rally Exce pt i on
However, Commissioner Kronovet may nevertheless participate in the governmental
decision in which he has a disqualifying conflict-of-interest ii he meets the reqiirements of the
basic public generally exception under Regulation 19707.I.
Under that Regulation, he must meet a two-part test by showing that the decision wogld
affect a "significant segment" of the public in "substantially the ,o*. ,rorner" as it financially
affects his economic interest. For decisions that affect o p,,6li" official's real property,
"significant segment" is defined as l0-percent or more of all property owners or all residential


File No. A-10-05la
Page No.4
property owners in Santa Monica, or 5,000 property owners or residential propert y owners in the
clty.
Your facts indicate there are 22,061property owners in Santa Monica and 20,734
residential property owners. In addition, there are 3,036 owners of rent controlled properties,
and there are 492 six-unit, rent-controlled properties in the city.
Based on your facts, it appears that a "significant segment" will be affected by the annual
rent increase decision because l0 percent of all property owners in the city equal 2,2}6,while l0
percent of all residential property owners in the city equal 2,073. You indicaL that 3,036 owners
of rent controlled properties will be affected by this decision. Therefore you have identified a
significant segment of the public with respect to the governmental decision in question and this
factor is met.
The next step is to determine whether the "significant segment" will be affected in
substantially the same manner as Commissioner Kronovet's economic interest. A number of
factors are considered in determining whether a an economic interest in real property will be
financially affected in a manner substantially similar to the significant segmant including, but not
limited to: the location of the property, the impact on the property due to development potential,
the size of the property, and the number of units/parcels owned by the official compared to others in
the significant segment. (Regulation tB7O7.l(bX2)(A).)
Your facts indicate that Commissioner Kronovet owns a six-unit residential property and
there are 492 six-unit, rent-controlled properties in the city. In order to satisfy this piong oi th"
analysis, at least ten percent of the city's property owners or 10 percent of all residential-property
owners in the city must be impacted in substantially the same manner. (BergerAdvice Litter,
No. 4-05-054.)
Based on the facts you present it appears unlikely that Commissioner Kronovet's
economic interest in real property will be affected in "substantially the same manner,'as the
significant segment identified above (i.e. the 2,206 property owners in Santa Monica, or the
2,073 residential property owners in the city).5
Therefore, you have not provided us enough information to indicate that the public
generally exception would apply to Commissioner Kronovet's circumstances. Accordingly,
Commissioner Kronovet may not make, participate in making, or influence decisions r.luraing
the annual rent increase.
5 For instance. in the S/rire1' Advice Letter, No. 4-06- 132, we stated that "given the fact that there are two
properties owned by the Commissioner. it is unlikely that the dollar value impact n.ith.r. two properties will be
substantially he same as on property owners' homes where they only own one home."


File No. A-10-051a
Page No. 5
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By:
Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question: Based on the new facts provided, you ask reconsideration with regard to whether: (1) The presumption of non-materiality for commissioner Kronovet's real property interest is rebutted pursuant to Regulation 18705.2(b) due to the possible effects on the income-producing potential of the real property; (2) Whether the public generally exception applies to Commissioner Kronovet's circumstances; (3) whether commissioner Kronovet's waiver of the 2010/2011 General Adjustment has any effect on his legal obligation to disqualify himself from the annual rent increase decision.
sections.conclusion: Therefore, reviewing the additional facts you present and the applicable law, we reaffirm the conclusion reached in the Jones Advice Letter, supra that that Commissioner Kronovet has a disqualifying conflict of interest and may not make, participate in making, or influence any government decisions allowing residential rental property owners in the city on annual rent increase because the decision would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on his economic interest in six residential rental units. However, Commissioner Kronovet may nevertheless participate in the governmental decision in which he has a disqualifying conflict-of-interest if he meets the requirements of the basic public generally exception under Regulation 19707.1. However, based on your facts, you have not provided us enough information to indicate that the public generally exception would apply to Commissioner Kronovet's circumstances. Accordingly, Commissioner Kronovet may not make, participate in making, or influence decisions regarding the annual rent increase.

a
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Santa Monica Rent Control Board Commissioner Kronovet must disqualify himself from voting on annual rent increases for his six residential rental units, considering his signed waiver of the 2010/2011 adjustment, the presumption of materiality under regulations, and whether the public generally exception applies to his circumstances.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Commissioner Kronovet has a disqualifying conflict of interest and cannot vote on annual rent increase decisions because the decision would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his six residential rental properties. The signed waiver does not eliminate the conflict because the rental adjustment decision would still impact property values. The public generally exception does not apply because his economic interest (six-unit property) would not be affected in substantially the same manner as the broader segment of all property owners in the city.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR document with minor formatting issues. This is a reconsideration letter (A-10-051A) responding to an earlier advice letter (A-10-051). Clear question and conclusion sections present. Some OCR artifacts noted (e.g., 'PRACI'TCES' for 'PRACTICES', 'odifi6i11.nt' for 'ordinance') but meaning is recoverable. The letter references another concurrent advice letter (Kronovet A-10-093) and prior correspondence.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Based on the new facts provided, you ask reconsideration with regard to whether: (1) The presumption of non-materiality for commissioner Kronovet's real property interest is rebutted pursuant to Regulation 18705.2(b) due to the possible effects on the income-producing potential of the real property; (2) Whether the public generally exception applies to Commissioner Kronovet's circumstances; (3) whether commissioner Kronovet's waiver of the 2010/2011 General Adjustment has any effect on his legal obligation to disqualify himself from the annual rent increase decision.

CONCLUSION: Therefore, reviewing the additional facts you present and the applicable law, we reaffirm the conclusion reached in the Jones Advice Letter, supra that that Commissioner Kronovet has a disqualifying conflict of interest and may not make, participate in making, or influence any government decisions allowing residential rental property owners in the city on annual rent increase because the decision would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on his economic interest in six residential rental units. However, Commissioner Kronovet may nevertheless participate in the governmental decision in which he has a disqualifying conflict-of-interest if he meets the requirements of the basic public generally exception under Regulation 19707.1. However, based on your facts, you have not provided us enough information to indicate that the public generally exception would apply to Commissioner Kronovet's circumstances. Accordingly, Commissioner Kronovet may not make, participate in making, or influence decisions regarding the annual rent increase.

embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter reconsiders a conflict of interest matter regarding Santa Monica Rent Control Board Commissioner Kronovet's participation in annual rent increase decisions affecting his six residential rental units. The Commission reaffirms that Kronovet must disqualify himself due to a material financial conflict and finds that the public generally exception does not apply to his circumstances.
